title: Madison Bailey
comment: Undid revision [[Special:Diff/1345448554|1345448554]] by [[Special:Contributions/~2026-18734-98|~2026-18734-98]] ([[User talk:~2026-18734-98|talk]]) -- unexplained reference removal
diff_added:
==Career==
Bailey originally wanted to be a singer, but transitioned to acting and modeling at age 15 after suffering [[stage fright]] while singing in front of people.<ref>{{cite magazine|first=Sabrina|last=Abbas|url=https://www.lofficielusa.com/film-tv/madison-bailey-netflix-outer-banks|title=Meet Madison Bailey, the Breakout Star of Netflix's Gen Z Answer to 'The O.C.'|magazine=[[L'Officiel]]|date=April 23, 2020|access-date=January 18, 2021|archive-date=June 21, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200621144437/https://www.lofficielusa.com/film-tv/madison-bailey-netflix-outer-banks|url-status=live}}</ref> She began her professional acting career in 2015,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Casale |first1=Maddy |title=Madison Bailey Makes A Splash in 'Outer Banks' Season 3 |url=https://decider.com/2023/02/22/woman-crush-wednesday-madison-bailey/ |website=[[New York Post|Decider]] |access-date=March 29, 2025 |language=en |date=February 22, 2023}}</ref> appearing in TV series ''[[Mr. Mercedes (TV series)|Mr. Mercedes]]'' and ''[[Constantine (TV series)|Constantine]]''. In 2018, she was cast as Wendy Hernandez, a teenage [[metahuman]] with the ability to control and manipulate the air, in the [[The CW|CW]] science fiction TV series ''[[Black Lightning (TV series)|Black Lightning]]'', a role she played for two seasons.
diff_removed: ==Career==|url=https://decider.com/2023/02/22/woman-crush-wednesday-madison-bailey/ |website=[[New York Post|Decider]] |access-date=March 29, 2025 |language=en |date=February 22, 2023}}</ref> appearing in TV series ''[[Mr. Mercedes (TV series)|Mr. Mercedes]]'' and ''[[Constantine (TV series)|Constantine]]''. In 2018, she was cast as Wendy Hernandez, a teenage [[metahuman]] with the ability to control and manipulate the air, in the [[The CW|CW]] science fiction TV series ''[[Black Lightning (TV series)|Black Lightning]]'', a role she played for two seasons.

title: Rasmus Lindgren
diff_added:
Lindgren has two siblings. His father, Bernt Göran Lindgren, is a former professional [[ice hockey]] player. Lindgren later said in an interview that he considered Netherlands to be his second home.{{cite news|title=Rasmus Lindgren: 'Nederland is mijn tweede vaderland geworden'|url=https://www.dvhn.nl/sport/Rasmus-Lindgren-%E2%80%98Nederland-is-mijn-tweede-vaderland-geworden%E2%80%99-21317236.html|publisher=Dagblad Noorden|language=Swedish|date=30 April 2016|access-date=8 January 2021}} Growing up, Lindgren was friends with teammate [[Alexander Farnerud]].{{cite news|title=Kompisarnas möte i derbyt|url=https://www.expressen.se/gt/sport/fotboll/allsvenskan/kompisarnas-mote-i-derbyt/|publisher=Expressen|language=Swedish|date=22 November 2020|access-date=8 January 2021}} Lindgren is married and together, they have two children.{{cite news|title=Rasmus Lindgren klubblös|url=https://www.landskronadirekt.com/2012/09/26/rasmus-lindgren-klubblos/|publisher=Landskrona Direkt|language=Swedish|date=26 September 2012|access-date=8 January 2021}}

title: Alex (Street Fighter)
comment: /* Promotion and reception */ Replacing EventHubs with ShackNews
diff_added: Jack Coleman of ''[[TheGamer]]'' found the situation weird, pointing out that earlier story material for Alex suggested that he "loved her like a sister". However, he suggested the change to Tom and Alex's relationship may have been due to societal norms in Japan, where often another family member takes in a child instead of a friend of the family. He added that while Patricia was Alex's second cousin, they did not count as blood relatives, but still expressed that them being adopted siblings was odd enough and questioned the necessity to make them related.<ref>{{cite web |first=Jack |last=Coleman |accessdate=2026-03-18 |date=2026-03-18 |url=https://www.thegamer.com/street-fighter-6-alex-patricia-cousin-sister-love-interest/ |title=Street Fighter 6 Decided To Make Alex's Love Interest His Cousin-Sister, For Some Reason |website=[[TheGamer]]}}</ref> Isaiah Colbert of ''[[Aftermath (website)|Aftermath]]'' found the changes to Alex and Patricia's relationship extremely unnecessary, calling it "grounds for [[character assassination]] for anyone who had him as their main", and expressing that them being adopted siblings was weird on its own and only made worse by them being related.<ref>{{cite web | last=Colbert | first=Isaiah | title=I Don't Like How Alex Street Fighter 6 Uses His Free Will | website=Aftermath | date=March 23, 2026 | url=https://aftermath.site/i-dont-like-how-alex-street-fighter-6-uses-his-free-will/ | access-date=March 23, 2026}}</ref> The game's director, Takayuki Nakayama, released a statement apologizing for any confusion regarding Alex's story and said they "plan to revise certain text passages that may have been misleading in the near future."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Khan |first1=Asif |title=SF6 Game Director Takayuki Nakayama apologizes for confusion around Alex's story, revisions coming |url=https://www.shacknews.com/article/148473/sf6-alex-dlc-story-devs-retconned |website=ShackNews |access-date=26 March 2026}}</ref>
diff_removed: Jack Coleman of ''[[TheGamer]]'' found the situation weird, pointing out that earlier story material for Alex suggested that he "loved her like a sister". However, he suggested the change to Tom and Alex's relationship may have been due to societal norms in Japan, where often another family member takes in a child instead of a friend of the family. He added that while Patricia was Alex's second cousin, they did not count as blood relatives, but still expressed that them being adopted siblings was odd enough and questioned the necessity to make them related.<ref>{{cite web |first=Jack |last=Coleman |accessdate=2026-03-18 |date=2026-03-18 |url=https://www.thegamer.com/street-fighter-6-alex-patricia-cousin-sister-love-interest/ |title=Street Fighter 6 Decided To Make Alex's Love Interest His Cousin-Sister, For Some Reason |website=[[TheGamer]]}}</ref> Isaiah Colbert of ''[[Aftermath (website)|Aftermath]]'' found the changes to Alex and Patricia's relationship extremely unnecessary, calling it "grounds for [[character assassination]] for anyone who had him as their main", and expressing that them being adopted siblings was weird on its own and only made worse by them being related.<ref>{{cite web | last=Colbert | first=Isaiah | title=I Don't Like How Alex Street Fighter 6 Uses His Free Will | website=Aftermath | date=March 23, 2026 | url=https://aftermath.site/i-dont-like-how-alex-street-fighter-6-uses-his-free-will/ | access-date=March 23, 2026}}</ref> The game's director, Takayuki Nakayama, released a statement apologizing for any confusion regarding Alex's story and said they "plan to revise certain text passages that may have been misleading in the near future."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hills |first1=Dakota |title=Street Fighter 6 Director apologizes for confusion over Alex's controversial marriage, update coming to revise story |url=https://www.eventhubs.com/news/2026/mar/25/street-fighter-alex-story-revise/ |website=eventhubs |access-date=26 March 2026}}</ref>

title: Edwin C. Fairchild
diff_added: A long-term member of the [[Social Democratic Federation]] (SDF), Fairchild was a member of its radical Central [[Metropolitan Borough of Hackney|Hackney]] branch, alongside [[Zelda Kahan]] and [[Theodore Rothstein]]. As early as 1909, he was a signatory to a resolution denouncing party leader [[H. M. Hyndman]]'s anti-German rhetoric.<ref>Martin Crick, ''The History of the Social-Democratic Federation'', p.232</ref> He supported closer links with the [[Independent Labour Party]] and other socialists, and worked with [[Alf Purcell]] and [[Victor Grayson]] on the Provisional Committee for the Promotion of Common Ground Among the Socialists. This was opposed by the right wing of the SDF, but proved successful, as it constituted the [[British Socialist Party]] (BSP), and the SDF merged itself into the new party.<ref>Martin Crick, ''The History of the Social-Democratic Federation'', pp.238-240</ref>
diff_removed: A long-term member of the [[Social Democratic Federation]] (SDF), Fairchild was a member of its radical Central [[Metropolitan Borough of Hackney|Hackney]] branch, alongside [[Zelda Kahan]] and [[Theodore Rothstein]]. As early as 1909, he was a signatory to a resolution denouncing party leader [[H. M. Hyndman]]'s anti-German rhetoric.<ref>Martin Crick, ''The History of the Social-Democratic Federation'', p.232</ref> He supported closer links with the [[Independent Labour Party]] and other socialists, and worked with [[Alf Purcell]] and [[Victor Grayson]] on the Provisional Committee for the Promotion of Common Ground Among the Socialists. This was opposed by the right-wing of the SDF, but proved successful, as it constituted the [[British Socialist Party]] (BSP), and the SDF merged itself into the new party.<ref>Martin Crick, ''The History of the Social-Democratic Federation'', pp.238-240</ref>

title: Isaac Klein
comment: /* Role within Conservative Judaism */ replaced: right-wing → right wing
diff_added: Klein was a leader of the right wing of the Conservative movement. He was president of the [[Rabbinical Assembly]], 1958–1960, and a member of its Committee on Jewish Law and Standards, 1948–1979. He was the author of several books, notably, ''A Guide to Jewish Religious Practice''. One of the outstanding halakhists of the movement, he served as a leading member of the [[Committee on Jewish Law and Standards]] from 1948 until his death in 1979.
diff_removed: Klein was a leader of the right-wing of the Conservative movement. He was president of the [[Rabbinical Assembly]], 1958–1960, and a member of its Committee on Jewish Law and Standards, 1948–1979. He was the author of several books, notably, ''A Guide to Jewish Religious Practice''. One of the outstanding halakhists of the movement, he served as a leading member of the [[Committee on Jewish Law and Standards]] from 1948 until his death in 1979.

title: Rebecca Blumhagen

title: National Science Appreciation Day
diff_added: National Science Appreciation Day has been established by the [[Center for Inquiry]]'s ScienceSaves campaign in 2022.<ref>{{Cite web |title=National Science Appreciation Day |url=https://www.daysoftheyear.com/days/national-science-appreciation-day/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231128034149/https://www.daysoftheyear.com/days/national-science-appreciation-day/ |archive-date=28 November 2023 |access-date=28 November 2023 |website=Days of the Year}}</ref> As of 2023, ten States had adopted official proclamations recognizing the commemoration: [[Arizona]], [[Illinois]], [[Kansas]], [[Louisiana]], [[New Hampshire]], [[New Mexico]], [[Oklahoma]], [[Virginia]], [[Washington (state)|Washington]] and [[Wisconsin]], as well as [[Washington, D.C.|District of Columbia]].<ref name=SciS>{{Cite web |title=National Science Appreciation Day |url=https://sciencesaves.org/about-national-science-appreciation-day/#:~:text=Marked%20annually%20on%20March%2026th,the%20importance%20of%20critical%20thinking |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231012005350/https://sciencesaves.org/about-national-science-appreciation-day/#:~:text=Marked%20annually%20on%20March%2026th,the%20importance%20of%20critical%20thinking |archive-date=12 October 2023 |access-date=28 November 2023 |website=ScienceSaves| date=27 April 2023 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Fidalgo |first=Paul |date=16 March 2022 |title=Ten States Plus D.C. Are First to Issue National Science Appreciation Day Proclamations |url=https://centerforinquiry.org/press_releases/nine-states-plus-d-c-are-first-to-issue-national-science-appreciation-day-proclamations/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231128041043/https://centerforinquiry.org/press_releases/nine-states-plus-d-c-are-first-to-issue-national-science-appreciation-day-proclamations/ |archive-date=28 November 2023 |access-date=28 November 2023 |website=Center for Inquiry}}</ref> By 2025, many more states had issued proclamations, including [[Arkansas]], [[Colorado]], [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]], [[Idaho]], [[Kentucky]], [[Massachusetts]], [[Michigan]], [[Minnesota]], [[North Carolina]], [[North Dakota]], [[Oregon]], [[South Dakota]], and [[Vermont]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Staff |title=PRESS RELEASE: Oklahoma joins in celebration of fourth annual National Science Appreciation Day |url=https://freepressokc.com/press-release-oklahoma-joins-in-celebration-of-fourth-annual-national-science-appreciation-day/ |website=Oklahoma City Free Press |publisher=Oklahoma City Free Press LLC |access-date=10 May 2025}}</ref>

title: Marcus Jastrow
comment: /* Religious views */ replaced: right-wing → right wing
diff_added: Along with [[Benjamin Szold]] and [[Frederick de Sola Mendes]], Marcus Jastrow was characterized by Jewish historian [[Jacob Rader Marcus]] as being on the right wing of early American [[Reform Judaism|Reform]].<ref name=MarcusUSJewry>{{cite book|last=Marcus|first=Jacob Rader|title=United States Jewry, 1776-1985|year=1989|publisher=Wayne State University Press|page=649}}</ref> While opposed to the [[Pittsburgh Platform]], he allowed an organ to be installed in the Rodeph Shalom Congregation.

title: National Front (UK)
comment: /* Early growth: 1968–1972 */ replaced: right-wing → right wing
diff_added: The party held its first annual conference in October 1967; it was picketed by [[anti-fascist]]s.{{sfn|Walker|1977|p=84}} In 1968, Chesterton's leadership was unsuccessfully challenged by Fountaine, who then left the party.{{sfnm|1a1=Walker|1y=1977|1pp=86–87|2a1=Fielding|2y=1981|2p=24|3a1=Thurlow|3y=1987|3pp=279–280|4a1=Sykes|4y=2005|4p=106}} There were further internal arguments after its lease on its [[Westminster]] headquarters ended. Ex-LEL members wanted another base in central London, while the ex-GBM and ex-BNP factions favoured moving into the GBM's old headquarters in [[Tulse Hill]]. Chesterton backed the ex-LEL position, and offered a small office in [[Fleet Street]].{{sfn|Walker|1977|pp=89–90}} In April 1968, immigration became the foremost political topic in the national media after the [[Conservative Party (UK)|Conservative Party]] politician [[Enoch Powell]] made his [[Rivers of Blood speech]], an appeal against non-white immigration into Britain.{{sfnm|1a1=Walker|1y=1977|1p=109|2a1=Taylor|2y=1982|2pp=20–21|3a1=Thurlow|3y=1987|3p=276|4a1=Eatwell|4y=2003|4p=337}} Although Powell proposed more moderate measures for expelling migrants than the NF, his use of language was similar to theirs,{{sfn|Walker|1977|p=113}} and some individuals on the right wing of the Conservatives defected to the NF.{{sfn|Walker|1977|p=115}}

title: 2026 Port Shuaiba drone attack
diff_added:
The husband of a soldier killed in the attack described the targeted structure at the operations center as a "shipping container-style building".<ref name=":3" /> It was a triple-wide [[Trailer (vehicle)|trailer]] with an office interior surrounded by reinforced concrete [[T-Wall]]s, a standard US military setup.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":2" /> A photo from 2021 shows the trailer had a "thin metal rooftop", and no significant upgrades to its protection since at least 2009. Military officials told ''[[The Washington Post]]'' there was no additional reinforcements to the structure.<ref name=":1" /> The military presence in Port Shuaiba did not possess [[Counter Rocket, Artillery, and Mortar|counter]] [[Counter Rocket, Artillery, and Mortar|rocket, artillery and mortar]] systems and "basically had no drone defeat capability" despite requests for further support according to internal sources speaking to [[CBS News]]. Because of these factors, the military was uncertain as to if the operations center should be used.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=LaPorta |first=James |date=2026-03-03 |title=Military questioned use of makeshift office space in Kuwait where U.S. troops were killed |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/iran-strike-kuwait-officials-question-fortifications/ |access-date=2026-03-11 |website=[[CBS News]] |language=en-US |archive-date=2026-03-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260309174140/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/iran-strike-kuwait-officials-question-fortifications/ |url-status=live |author-link=James LaPorta}}</ref>
Captain Tim Hawkins confirmed an investigation was being conducted into the incident.<ref name=":3" /> CBS News reported on March 5 that according to the preliminary conclusions of a [[United States Army Central|US Army Central]] memo reviewed by the agency, Iranian intelligence and allied militant groups had likely managed to track and monitor the transfer of US military personnel to smaller military installations a week prior to the attack. The memo reported the discovery of "GPS transponders connected to balloons or parachutes" near [[MIM-104 Patriot]] systems in the area on March 2. Military officials speaking to CBS News had attested to spotting [[quadcopter]] reconnaissance flights across Port Shuaiba in the preceding week.<ref name=":0" />
Speaking on the attack at a press conference, Secretary of Defense [[Pete Hegseth]] said the successful penetration of US air defense systems was an outlier, acknowledging drones as a "powerful weapon" and pledging to "prosecute the remainder of this operation in a manner that honors" the victims. Department of Defense spokesperson [[Sean Parnell (spokesperson)|Sean Parnell]] discredited media descriptions of the operations center as makeshift, claiming it was adequately fortified. He maintained that "Every possible measure has been taken to safeguard our troops — at every level."<ref name=":2" />

title: Nisbet Provincial Forest
diff_added: The '''Nisbet Provincial Forest''' is a provincially protected mixed-wood forest surrounded by [[Aspen parkland]] in North Central [[Saskatchewan]], [[Canada]]. It consists of a north block north of the city of [[Prince Albert, Saskatchewan]], and a south block between [[Duck Lake, Saskatchewan]], [[MacDowall, Saskatchewan]] and [[Lily Plain, Saskatchewan]]. The forest drew [[Métis people (Canada)|Métis]] and white settlers to the area during the 1860s to 1880s and was an important source of building materials and fuel in this period. Today it is a provincially protected area although [[cattle]] [[grazing]] and recreational use including [[cross country skiing]], [[snowmobiling]], [[hiking]], and [[hunting]] in the area are permitted.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2012-01-06 |title=NISBET INTEGRATED FOREST LAND USE PLAN |url=https://publications.saskatchewan.ca/#/products/24502 |access-date=2026-03-26 |website=Saskatchewan}}</ref>

title: Alf Skowron
diff_added: Skowron was on the right wing of the New Democratic Party.  In 1979, he led council opposition to the introduction of bilingual [[English (language)|English]] and [[French (language)|French]] municipal signs.  The signs were introduced to recognize the legal rights of Manitoba's francophone community; Skowron argued that they privileged one particular ethnic group.<ref>"Debate on French signs inflames city passions", ''Globe and Mail'', 14 April 1979, P8.</ref>  When he ran for mayor later in the same year, he sought but did not receive the provincial NDP's official endorsement.  He accused the party of being infiltrated with [[communist]]s, citing that Communist [[Joseph Zuken]] had emerged as the main left-of-centre candidate.<ref>"Deputy, Communist two top contenders in Winnipeg election", ''Winnipeg Free Press'', 20 June 1979, P9.</ref>
diff_removed: Skowron was on the right-wing of the New Democratic Party. title: November 2025 visit by Ahmed al-Sharaa to the United States
diff_added: Analyst Hazem Salem Dmour of the Strategies [[Think Tank]] stated that al-Sharaa’s invitation to the White House was described by a broad spectrum of American political leaders as at least ill-considered and, off the record, as potentially un-American. He noted that the visit occurred within the context of a troubled U.S. foreign policy that, in his view, was inconsistent with [[Americanism (ideology)|American values]], particularly given that the shared issues between Syria and the United States did not necessitate inviting al-Sharaa to the White House.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Real clear world: A former terrorist inside the white house |url=https://strategiecs.com/en/analyses/real-clear-world-a-former-terrorist-inside-the-white-house |access-date=2026-03-26 |website=strategiecs.com |language=en}}</ref>
diff_removed:
Analyst Hazem Salem Dmour of the Strategies [[Think Tank]] stated that al-Sharaa’s invitation to the White House was described by a broad spectrum of American political leaders as at least ill-considered and, off the record, as potentially un-American. He noted that the visit occurred within the context of a troubled U.S. foreign policy that, in his view, was inconsistent with [[Americanism (ideology)|American values]], particularly given that the shared issues between Syria and the United States did not necessitate inviting al-Sharaa to the White House.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Real clear world: A former terrorist inside the white house |url=https://strategiecs.com/en/analyses/real-clear-world-a-former-terrorist-inside-the-white-house |access-date=2026-03-26 |website=strategiecs.com |language=en}}</ref>

[[Amin Saikal]], writing in [[The Conversation (website)|The Conversation]], noted that two prominent young [[Muslims|Muslim]] figures experienced a notable rise to power in the same year: Al-Sharaa in Syria and [[Zohran Mamdani]] in [[New York City]]. He observed that Trump embraced al-Sharaa, a former rebel-turned-president, by inviting him to the White House, while Mamdani, a popularly elected mayor, was not extended similar recognition. He added that the contrasting trajectories of al-Sharaa and Mamdani illustrate the perceived contradictions in U.S. policy under Trump and highlight an emerging world order lacking rules-based principles.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Saikal |first=Amin |date=2025-11-12 |title=One is a former al-Qaeda leader, the other a US mayor. Guess which was invited to the White House |url=https://theconversation.com/one-is-a-former-al-qaeda-leader-the-other-a-us-mayor-guess-which-was-invited-to-the-white-house-269490 |access-date=2026-03-26 |website=The Conversation |language=en-US}}</ref>

title: Delwyn Williams
comment: /* Political career */ replaced: right-wing → right wing
diff_added: Williams was elected to the Montgomeryshire County Council.<ref name = Times/> He unsuccessfully fought the [[1970 United Kingdom general election]],{{citation needed|date = September 2024}} and the [[February 1974 United Kingdom election|February 1974]] elections, the latter at [[Ceredigion (UK Parliament constituency)|Cardiganshire]].<ref name = Times/> He was elected [[Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)|member of parliament]] for the traditionally Liberal seat of [[Montgomeryshire (UK Parliament constituency)|Montgomeryshire]] in [[1979 United Kingdom general election|1979]], ousting [[Emlyn Hooson]] by a majority of 1,593 votes. He lost the seat by 668 votes after one term to the Liberal [[Alex Carlile, Baron Carlile of Berriew|Alex Carlile]] in [[1983 United Kingdom general election|1983]]. He was on the right wing of the party, and a strong supporter of [[Margaret Thatcher]].<ref name = Times/> After leaving parliament, he continued to work as a solicitor.<ref name = Times/>

title: Pierre Cot
diff_added: The head of his ministerial office, [[Jean Moulin]] (later a leader of the [[French Resistance]]), made several trips to Spain. This brought Cot into close collaboration with the communists, with whom he had increasingly sympathised since a visit to the [[Soviet Union]] in 1933. His activities were one of the factors leading to the withdrawal of the right wing of the Radical Party from the government and Blum's resignation in June 1937. In Blum's second government in March and April 1938, Cot was Minister for Commerce.

title: Melvin Moti
diff_added:
'''Melvin Moti''' (born 1977) is a [[Dutch people|Dutch]] contemporary video and media artist who examines neurological, scientific and historic processes in relation to [[visual culture]].<ref name=":7" /> His work explores ideas of human experiences of spirituality, space, time and consciousness.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Spectral Spectrum: ABN Amro Kunstprijs 2015|last=Barnas|first=Maria|year=2015|isbn=978-90-816713-2-3|location=Amsterdam}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=Yes, wow, totally, pointful|last=Rehberg|first=Vivian Sky|publisher=Rehberg|year=2012|isbn=978-90-818302-0-1|pages=18}}</ref>
In ''No Show,'' the viewer is tempted to use his own imagination: in this film, the spectator hears a tour being conducted at the [[Hermitage Museum|Leningrad Hermitage]], alongside static images of emptied museum galleries.<ref>[https://www.stedelijk.nl/en/collection/50779-melvin-moti-no-show "No Show"] on Stedelijk Museum. Retrieved 20 December 2019. </ref> During the [[World War II|Second World War]], the Hermitage was evacuated of artworks due to the imminent threat of bombing. ''No Show'' tells the story of a young museum employee who allegedly continued to give tours during that period. The film's narrator describes the absent works of art in great detail to attempt to evoke vivid pictures.<ref name=":1" />
''Spectral Spectrum'' is an exhibition developed by Moti for the ABN AMRO Art Award.<ref name=":9">"Winner of the ABN AMRO 5th Art Award: Melvin Moti", exhibition [https://www.abnamro.com/en/images/Documents/010_About_ABN_AMRO/Art_and_historical_collections/Art_Award_-_Melvin_Moti.pdf Spectral Spectrum]. Retrieved from ABN AMRO on 20 December 2019. </ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite news|url=https://www.volkskrant.nl/cultuur-media/kijken-wordt-denken-bij-melvin-moti~b12c65d4/|title=Kijken wordt denken bij Melvin Moti|last=Bronwasser|first=Sacha|date=10 December 2015|work=Volkskrant|access-date=7 January 2020}}</ref> Moti explores ideas about time, space, perception and consciousness. His starting point was the early work of the Dutch artist [[JCJ Vanderheyden]] (1928-2012). Vanderheyden painted curved and crooked horizons onto narrow piles of wood, depicting the view from an airplane window and referring to an infinite empty space.<ref>"[https://www.stedelijk.nl/nl/collectie/11879-jcj-vanderheyden-himal-mountains Himal Mountains]" by JCJ Vanderheyden on Stedelijk Museum Amsterdam. Accessed on 20 december 2020.</ref> The airplane window itself appears in part, but also functions as a frame lending itself to the image before the painting is completed. When spectators would tilt their heads, the world moved in a similar fashion, foregrounding perspective and perception within the works.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dewitteraaf.be/artikel/detail/nl/4216|title=Melvin Moti: Spectral Spectrum|date=January 2016|website=De Witte Raaf|access-date=20 December 2019}}</ref> In his displaying of Vanderheyden's works in the context of his own work and

title: Najwa Karam
diff_added:
'''Najwa Karam''' ({{langx|ar|نجوى كرم}}; {{IPA|apc-LB|ˈnaʒwa ˈkaɾam}}) (born 26 February 1966) is a Lebanese [[Singing|singer]], [[songwriter]], [[Record producer|producer]], and [[Celebrity|television personality]]. She has sold an estimated 60 million records and is one of the highest-selling recording artists in the [[Middle East]].<ref name=":0">[http://www.arabianbusiness.com/the-world-s-100-most-powerful-arab-women-541034.html?itemid=541029 100 Most Powerful Arab Women] ''Arabian Business''</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Ham |first1=Anthony |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=u_DFlzttxl0C&pg=PA411 |title=Middle East |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |year=2010 |isbn=9781742203591}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Mughal |first=Waqar |title=Najwa Karam |url=https://www.forbesmiddleeast.com/lists/arab-music-stars-2021/najwa-karam/ |access-date=2026-01-22 |website=Forbes Lists |language=en-US}}</ref>
Other albums she released in the ‘90s include ''[[Naghmet Hob]] (“Melody of  love”)'', ''Ma Bassmahlak (“I won't let you")'', ''[[Maghroumeh]] (“In Love”),'' and ''Rouh Rouhi (“Soul of my soul”)'' . In 2000, Karam's tenth album, ''[[Oyoun Qalbi]] (“Eyes of my heart”)'' became her highest-selling album.
Karam learned to sing at a young age. She attended an evangelical secondary school and later earned a bachelor's degree in [[philosophy]]. Karam taught [[geography]] and [[Arabic]] at Eastern College in [[Zahlé]], Lebanon, for two years.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The official lebanese Top 20 - Najwa Karam |url=http://www.olt20.com/Najwa-Karam |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20251231155357/http://www.olt20.com/Najwa-Karam |archive-date=2025-12-31 |access-date=2026-02-25 |website=The official lebanese Top 20}}</ref>

title: Isabel Auld
comment: restored some removed context, minor copy edits, trimmed some wording
diff_added:
'''Isabel George Auld''' {{post-nominals|CM|country=CAN}} (née '''Hutcheson''' 21 September 1917 – 27 March 2016) was the [[chancellor (education)|chancellor]] of the [[University of Manitoba]] from 1977 to 1986. Before becoming the first woman to hold this position, Auld primarily worked as a volunteer from the 1940s to 1960s. During this time period, she co-founded the [[Consumers' Association of Canada]] in 1953 and became the association's president in 1964. Leading up to 1977, Auld was part of the board of directors for the University of Manitoba. Following her career, Auld became a [[Member of the Order of Canada]] in 1989. She also received the [[Queen Elizabeth II Golden Jubilee Medal]] in 2002 and the [[Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal]] in 2012.
Isabel Hutcheson was born in [[Winnipeg]], [[Manitoba]] on 21 September 1917.<ref name=Auld2016>{{cite news |last1=Auld |first1=Cathy |title=Lives Lived: Isabel G. Auld, 98 |url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/life/facts-and-arguments/lives-lived-isabel-g-auld-98/article32008170/ |access-date=15 February 2021 |work=The Globe and Mail |date=23 September 2016 |archive-date=11 October 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161011204820/http://www.theglobeandmail.com/life/facts-and-arguments/lives-lived-isabel-g-auld-98/article32008170/ |url-status=live }}</ref> After moving to [[Regina, Saskatchewan]] during her youth, she went to the [[University of Saskatchewan]] for her post-secondary education.<ref name=honoarysaskatchewan>{{cite web |title=Honorary Degrees - Isabel George Auld |url=https://library.usask.ca/archives/campus-history/honorary-degrees.php?id=360&view=detail&keyword=&campuses= |website=University of Saskatchewan |at=University Archives & Special Collections |access-date=15 February 2021 |archive-date=23 January 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123012618/https://library.usask.ca/archives/campus-history/honorary-degrees.php?id=360&view=detail&keyword=&campuses= |url-status=live }}</ref> While specializing in biology and genetics, Hutcheson earned both her Bachelor and Master of Science at Saskatchewan by 1940.<ref name=Manitobafonds>{{cite web |title=Isabel G. Auld fonds |url=https://umanitoba.ca/libraries/units/archives/collections/rad/auld.html |website=University of Manitoba |access-date=15 February 2021 |archive-date=25 March 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210325081535/https://umanitoba.ca/libraries/units/archives/collections/rad/auld.html |url-status=live }}</ref> She also went to [[McGill University]] and studied [[cytology]] for her postgraduate education.<ref name=Auld2016/> She received an honorary [[Doctor of Laws]] by both the University of Saskatchewan in 1979 and the [[University of Manitoba]] in 1986.<ref name=honoarysaskatchewan/><ref>{{cite web |title=Honorary Degree recipients |url=https://umanitoba.ca/governance/honours/honorary-degrees |website=University of Manitoba |at=1986 Isabel George Hutcheson Auld |access-da

title: Azania (Somalia)
diff_added: '''Azania''' ({{langx|so|Azaaniya}}, {{langx|ar|{{big|أزانيا}}}}), officially the '''Republic of Azania''' ({{langx|so|Dawladda Azaaniya}}), was a self-proclaimed [[Autonomous administrative division|autonomous state]] of [[Jubaland]] in southern [[Somalia]] that existed from 2011 to 2013. A group of Somali politicians proclaimed the creation of Azania on 3 April 2011 in [[Nairobi]], with [[Mohamed Abdi Mohamed]] as President. [[Abdiaziz Osman Mohamud]] as Vice President. The state's main intentions were to contest [[Al-Shabaab (militant group)|al-Shabaab]], which largely controlled Jubaland.
diff_removed: '''Azania''' ({{langx|so|Azaaniya}}, {{langx|ar|{{big|أزانيا}}}}), officially the '''Republic of Azania''' ({{langx|so|Dawladda Azaaniya}}), was a self-proclaimed [[Autonomous administrative division|autonomous state]] of [[Jubaland]] in southern [[Somalia]] that existed from 2011 to 2013. A group of Somali politicians proclaimed the creation of Azania on 3 April 2011 in [[Nairobi]], with [[Mohamed Abdi Mohamed]] as its President. The state's main intentions were to contest [[Al-Shabaab (militant group)|al-Shabaab]], which largely controlled Jubaland.
